In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'ja'} [[JA:御笏神社]] {{wikidata link|Q110915859}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = {{ruby|Oshaku Shrine|おしゃくじんじゃ}} |画像 = [[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '静岡県下田市大賀茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:御笏神社]] {{wikidata link|Q110915859}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = {{ruby|Oshaku Shrine|おしゃくじんじゃ}} |画像 = [[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '静岡県下田市大賀茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:御笏神社]] {{wikidata link|Q110915859}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = {{ruby|Oshaku Shrine|おしゃくじんじゃ}} |画像 = [[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おしやくしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '静岡県下田市大賀茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:御笏神社]] {{wikidata link|Q110915859}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = {{ruby|Oshaku Shrine|おしゃくじんじゃ}} |画像 = [[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:東京都島嶼部の神社]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Shrines in the Islands of Tokyo Metropolis]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[File:Osyakujinja.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Kamitsuki|Q11590690|WD=Q11590690|lt=Kamitsuki|1=Kamitsuki}} |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) |社格 = former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = October 10 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=Oshaku Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oshaku Shrine''' (おしゃくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}. === History === Its founding period is unknown. However, because it is identified with the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} '''Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}, it is presumed to have already existed at least during the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} era ({{ill|lt=901|Q23580|WD=Q23580|1=901}}–{{ill|lt=923|Q30108|WD=Q30108|1=923}})Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005, p.246.. It was originally in the northeast of {{ill|lt=Miyakejima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}, but in {{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|WD=Q6618|1=1561}} ({{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 13) it was moved to its present location[https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Top page of Miyakejima shrines] Miyakejima shrines.. The shrine priests of this shrine have been inherited for generations by the {{ill|lt=Mibu clan|Q11429506|WD=Q11429506|1=Mibushi}}. The Mibu clan is known as the family that compiled the ''{{ill|lt=Miyake-ki|Q17215100|WD=Q17215100|1=Miyake-ki}}'', which records the origins of the {{ill|lt=Izu Islands|Q864302|WD=Q864302|1=Izu Islands}}. The shrine has many merged shrines, including several shikinaisha. In addition to the annual grand festival on October 10, in mid-July the Gozu Tennō Festival (so-called {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873|1=Gion Matsuri}}), derived from one merged shrine, {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} Shrine, is also held lively[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima (pp.66-68, p.84)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association. === Enshrined deity === * Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto (さきたまひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin). According to the mythology in ''Miyake-ki'', the three sister consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), worshiped at shrines on Miyakejima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}} and {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}}, rescued from a dragon (great serpent) and brought to Miyakejima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto at {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930277|WD=Q134930277|lt=Ninomiya Shrine|1=Ninomiya Shrine (Q134930277)}}, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto at Oshaku Shrine[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Survey of Engishiki Shrines. === Cultural properties === *Rituals of Oshaku Shrine (Tokyo-designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property, designated February 21, Shōwa 32)[https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=501 Rituals of Oshaku Shrine] Tokyo. === Access === *16 minutes by car from Sabigahama Port. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === *Tokyo Historical Education Research Society (ed.), ''Historical Walks of Tokyo, Lower Volume (Historical Walks 13): Tama and Islands'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2005. === External links === * [https://miyakejima-jinja.jimdofree.com/ Shrines of Miyakejima (Tomiga Shrine, Oshaku Shrine, Shiitori Shrine, etc.)] - Official site * [https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/osyakujinja/ Oshaku Shrine] - Miyakejima Tourism Association {{Pref-stub|pref=東京都}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oshaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Shrines in the Islands of Tokyo Metropolis]] [[Category:三宅村の建築物]] [[Category:三宅村の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Tokyo]]
